sentences.zh: 老子曰：「至治之極，鄰國相望，雞狗之聲相聞，民各甘其食，美其服，安其俗，樂其業，至老死，不相往來。」

translations.literal: Laozi said: 'At the height of perfect governance, neighboring states can see one another, the sounds of roosters and dogs can be heard between them, the people each relish their own food, admire their own clothes, feel at ease in their own customs, and take pleasure in their own work; they grow old and die without ever visiting one another.'
translations.idiomatic: Laozi said: 'In the most perfectly governed age, neighboring states are within sight of one another, the crowing of roosters and the barking of dogs can be heard across the border, the people savor their own food, take pride in their own clothing, rest content in their own customs, and delight in their own occupations — and they grow old and die without ever setting foot in one another's lands.'

sentences.zh: 必用此為務，輓近世涂民耳目，則幾無行矣。

translations.literal: If one insists on making this the guiding principle and tries to pull the modern world back by stopping up the people's eyes and ears, then it is virtually impossible to put into practice.
translations.idiomatic: If one insists on making this the governing principle, attempting to drag the modern world backward by stopping up the eyes and ears of the people, the task would prove virtually impossible.

sentences.zh: 太史公曰：夫神農以前，吾不知已。

translations.literal: The Grand Historian says: Of the ages before Shennong, I have no knowledge.
translations.idiomatic: The Grand Historian remarks: Of the ages before Shennong, I have no knowledge.

sentences.zh: 至若詩書所述虞夏以來，耳目欲極聲色之好，口欲窮芻豢之味，身安逸樂，而心誇矜輓能之榮使。

translations.literal: But as the Odes and Documents describe from the times of Yu and Xia onward, people's ears and eyes have sought the utmost pleasure in music and beauty, their mouths have craved the finest flavors of grain-fed and pasture-fed meats, their bodies have desired ease and comfort, and their hearts have swelled with pride in the glory and influence that ability can bring.
translations.idiomatic: But as the Odes and the Documents attest, from the time of Yu and Xia onward, the ears and eyes of the people have craved the ultimate in music and beauty, their palates have sought the finest flavors of grain-fed and pasture-raised meats, their bodies have desired ease and comfort, and their hearts have swelled with pride at the glory that ability and influence can bring.

sentences.zh: 俗之漸民久矣，雖戶說以眇論，終不能化。

translations.literal: These customs have permeated the people for a long time; even if one went door to door expounding subtle doctrines, one could never transform them.
translations.idiomatic: These habits have seeped into the people over such a long span of time that even going door to door preaching subtle doctrines could never change them.

sentences.zh: 故善者因之，其次利道之，其次教誨之，其次整齊之，最下者與之爭。

translations.literal: Therefore the best ruler works with the natural flow; the next best guides the people through profit; the next best instructs and admonishes them; the next best regulates and orders them; the worst competes against them.
translations.idiomatic: And so the wisest ruler works with the natural grain of things; the next best guides the people through the inducement of profit; the next best instructs and admonishes them; the next best imposes regulation and order upon them; and the very worst contends against them.

sentences.zh: 夫山西饒材、竹、穀、纑、旄、玉石；

translations.literal: Now, west of the mountains there is an abundance of timber, bamboo, grain, hemp, yak tails, and jade and stone;
translations.idiomatic: The region west of the mountains abounds in timber, bamboo, grain, hemp, yak tails, jade, and fine stone;

sentences.zh: 山東多魚、鹽、漆、絲、聲色；

translations.literal: east of the mountains there is much fish, salt, lacquer, silk, and music and beauty;
translations.idiomatic: the region east of the mountains produces abundant fish, salt, lacquer, silk, and entertainments of music and beauty;

sentences.zh: 江南出枏、梓、薑、桂、金、錫、連、丹沙、犀、瑁、珠璣、齒革；

translations.literal: south of the Yangtze produces nanmu timber, catalpa wood, ginger, cassia, gold, tin, lead, cinnabar, rhinoceros horn, tortoiseshell, pearls, ivory, and hides;
translations.idiomatic: the lands south of the Yangtze yield nanmu and catalpa wood, ginger, cassia, gold, tin, lead, cinnabar, rhinoceros horn, tortoiseshell, pearls, ivory, and hides;

sentences.zh: 龍門、碣石北多馬、牛、羊、旃裘、筋角；

translations.literal: north of Longmen and Jieshi there are many horses, cattle, sheep, felt garments, sinews, and horns;
translations.idiomatic: north of Longmen and Jieshi one finds horses, cattle, sheep, felt garments, sinews, and horns in abundance;

sentences.zh: 銅、鐵則千里往往山出棋置：此其大較也。

translations.literal: as for copper and iron, throughout a thousand li the mountains yield them, scattered about like pieces on a chessboard: these are the broad outlines.
translations.idiomatic: and copper and iron are found scattered throughout the mountains across a thousand li, dotted about like pieces on a chessboard. These are the broad outlines.

sentences.zh: 皆中國人民所喜好謠俗被服飲食奉生送死之具也。

translations.literal: All of these are the goods that the people of the Central States delight in — the stuff of their customs and songs, their clothing and food, their sustenance in life and provisions for death.
translations.idiomatic: All these are the goods in which the people of the Central States take delight — the substance of their customs, their clothing and food, their sustenance in life and their provisions for the dead.

sentences.zh: 故待農而食之，虞而出之，工而成之，商而通之。

translations.literal: Therefore they depend on farmers to produce food, foresters to extract raw materials, artisans to fashion goods, and merchants to circulate them.
translations.idiomatic: And so these goods depend on farmers to produce them, foresters to extract them, artisans to fashion them, and merchants to circulate them.

sentences.zh: 此寧有政教發徵期會哉？

translations.literal: Was this ever a matter of government edicts, levies, or appointed schedules?
translations.idiomatic: Was any of this ever brought about by government edicts, official levies, or appointed schedules?

sentences.zh: 人各任其能，竭其力，以得所欲。

translations.literal: Each person employs his own ability and exhausts his own strength to obtain what he desires.
translations.idiomatic: Each person simply employs his own abilities and exhausts his own strength to obtain what he desires.

sentences.zh: 故物賤之徵貴，貴之徵賤，各勸其業，樂其事，若水之趨下，日夜無休時，不召而自來，不求而民出之。

translations.literal: Therefore when goods are cheap, that is a sign they will become expensive; when expensive, a sign they will become cheap. Each person is encouraged in his own trade and takes pleasure in his own work, like water flowing downward day and night without rest — without being summoned they come of their own accord, without being asked the people produce of themselves.
translations.idiomatic: When goods are cheap, it is a sign that prices will rise; when expensive, that they will fall. Each person is spurred on in his own trade and takes delight in his own work. Like water flowing downhill, day and night without ceasing, goods come without being summoned, and the people produce them without being asked.

sentences.zh: 豈非道之所符，而自然之驗邪？

translations.literal: Is this not the sign of the Way, and the proof of what is natural?
translations.idiomatic: Is this not the very manifestation of the Way, and the proof of what arises naturally?

sentences.zh: 《周書》曰：「農不出則乏其食，工不出則乏其事，商不出則三寶絕，虞不出則財匱少。」

translations.literal: The Book of Zhou says: 'When farmers do not produce, food is scarce; when artisans do not produce, goods are scarce; when merchants do not trade, the three treasures are cut off; when foresters do not extract, resources are depleted.'
translations.idiomatic: The Book of Zhou says: 'When farmers do not produce, food grows scarce. When artisans do not produce, goods grow scarce. When merchants do not trade, the three treasures are cut off. When foresters do not extract, resources are depleted.'

sentences.zh: 財匱少而山澤不辟矣。

translations.literal: When resources are depleted and scarce, the mountains and marshes are not opened up.
translations.idiomatic: When resources are depleted, the mountains and marshlands are left unopened.

sentences.zh: 此四者，民所衣食之原也。

translations.literal: These four occupations are the source from which the people clothe and feed themselves.
translations.idiomatic: These four occupations are the wellspring from which the people draw their clothing and food.

sentences.zh: 原大則饒，原小則鮮。

translations.literal: When the source is large there is abundance; when the source is small there is scarcity.
translations.idiomatic: When the source is ample, there is abundance; when it is narrow, there is scarcity.

sentences.zh: 上則富國，下則富家。

translations.literal: Above, it enriches the state; below, it enriches the household.
translations.idiomatic: At the top, it enriches the state; at the bottom, it enriches the household.

sentences.zh: 貧富之道，莫之奪予，而巧者有餘，拙者不足。

translations.literal: The way of poverty and wealth — no one seizes or bestows it; yet the skillful have surplus, and the clumsy fall short.
translations.idiomatic: The path to wealth and poverty is not imposed by anyone from above. The skillful end up with a surplus; the clumsy are left wanting.

sentences.zh: 故太公望封於營丘，地澙鹵，人民寡，於是太公勸其女功，極技巧，通魚鹽，則人物歸之，繦至而輻湊。

translations.literal: Therefore when the Grand Duke Wang was enfeoffed at Yingqiu, the land was salty and alkaline and the people were few. Thereupon the Grand Duke encouraged women's handicrafts, perfected technical skills, and opened trade in fish and salt; then people and goods flowed to him, arriving in swaddling clothes and converging like the spokes of a wheel.
translations.idiomatic: When the Grand Duke Wang was enfeoffed at Yingqiu, the land was salty and barren and the people few. He encouraged women's handicrafts, perfected artisanal skills, and opened up trade in fish and salt. People and goods streamed toward him, arriving as if carried in swaddling clothes, converging like spokes toward the hub of a wheel.

sentences.zh: 故齊冠帶衣履天下，海岱之閒斂袂而往朝焉。

translations.literal: Thus Qi supplied caps, sashes, clothing, and shoes to all under Heaven, and between the sea and Mount Tai people gathered their sleeves and went to pay homage at its court.
translations.idiomatic: And so Qi came to supply caps, sashes, clothing, and shoes to the entire realm. From the shores of the sea to the slopes of Mount Tai, the people gathered their sleeves and hastened to pay homage at Qi's court.

sentences.zh: 其後齊中衰，管子修之，設輕重九府，則桓公以霸，九合諸侯，一匡天下；

translations.literal: Later, when Qi fell into decline, Guan Zhong restored it by establishing the system of light-and-heavy regulations and the Nine Treasuries; thereupon Duke Huan achieved hegemony, convened the feudal lords nine times, and brought order to all under Heaven;
translations.idiomatic: Later, when Qi fell into decline, Guan Zhong revived it by establishing the system of price regulation and the Nine Treasuries. With this, Duke Huan achieved hegemony, convened the feudal lords nine times, and brought order to the entire realm;

sentences.zh: 而管氏亦有三歸，位在陪臣，富於列國之君。

translations.literal: and Guan Zhong himself possessed the 'three returns,' held the rank of a mere vassal's vassal, yet was wealthier than the rulers of the feudal states.
translations.idiomatic: and Guan Zhong himself, though he held only the rank of a vassal's vassal, possessed the 'three returns' and amassed wealth exceeding that of the rulers of the feudal states.

sentences.zh: 是以齊富彊至於威、宣也。

translations.literal: Thus the wealth and power of Qi continued down to the reigns of Kings Wei and Xuan.
translations.idiomatic: And so the wealth and power of Qi endured all the way down to the reigns of Kings Wei and Xuan.

sentences.zh: 故曰：「倉廩實而知禮節，衣食足而知榮辱。」

translations.literal: Therefore it is said: 'When the granaries are full, the people come to know propriety and ritual; when clothing and food are sufficient, the people come to know honor and shame.'
translations.idiomatic: Thus the saying goes: 'When the granaries are full, the people learn propriety and ritual; when clothing and food are sufficient, the people learn honor and shame.'

sentences.zh: 禮生於有而廢於無。

translations.literal: Ritual is born from abundance and perishes from want.
translations.idiomatic: Ritual is born of plenty and withers in want.

sentences.zh: 故君子富，好行其德；

translations.literal: Therefore when a gentleman is wealthy, he delights in practicing virtue;
translations.idiomatic: When a gentleman grows wealthy, he takes delight in practicing virtue;

sentences.zh: 小人富，以適其力。

translations.literal: when a petty man is wealthy, he uses it to apply his strength.
translations.idiomatic: when a petty man grows wealthy, he channels it into the exercise of his strength.

sentences.zh: 淵深而魚生之，山深而獸往之，人富而仁義附焉。

translations.literal: Where the abyss is deep, fish are born; where the mountains are deep, beasts go; where people are wealthy, benevolence and righteousness attach themselves.
translations.idiomatic: Where the waters run deep, fish are born. Where the mountains are deep, beasts make their home. Where the people are wealthy, benevolence and righteousness attach themselves naturally.

sentences.zh: 富者得埶益彰，失埶則客無所之，以而不樂。

translations.literal: When the wealthy gain influence, their renown grows all the greater; when they lose influence, their guests have nowhere to go, and therefore they are unhappy.
translations.idiomatic: When the wealthy gain influence, their renown shines all the brighter. When they lose it, their guests have nowhere to turn, and discontent sets in.

sentences.zh: 夷狄益甚。

translations.literal: Among the barbarians this is all the more pronounced.
translations.idiomatic: Among the barbarian peoples, this is even more pronounced.

sentences.zh: 諺曰：「千金之子，不死於市。」

translations.literal: The proverb says: 'The son of a family worth a thousand in gold does not die in the marketplace.'
translations.idiomatic: As the proverb puts it: 'The son of a family worth a thousand in gold will never die in the marketplace.'

sentences.zh: 此非空言也。

translations.literal: This is no empty saying.
translations.idiomatic: This is no empty saying.

sentences.zh: 故曰：「天下熙熙，皆為利來；

translations.literal: Therefore it is said: 'All under heaven bustles and stirs — all come for the sake of profit;
translations.idiomatic: Thus it is said: 'All under heaven bustles and stirs — everyone comes for the sake of profit;

sentences.zh: 天下壤壤，皆為利往。」

translations.literal: all under heaven clamors and jostles — all go for the sake of profit.'
translations.idiomatic: all under heaven clamors and jostles — everyone goes for the sake of profit.'

sentences.zh: 夫千乘之王，萬家之侯，百室之君，尚猶患貧，而況匹夫編戶之民乎！

translations.literal: Now, kings of a thousand chariots, marquises of ten thousand households, lords of a hundred estates — even they still worry about poverty. How much more so the common man, a mere registered householder!
translations.idiomatic: If kings of a thousand chariots, marquises of ten thousand households, and lords of a hundred estates still worry about poverty, how much more so the common man, a mere registered householder!

sentences.zh: 昔者越王句踐困於會稽之上，乃用范蠡、計然。

translations.literal: In former times, King Goujian of Yue was trapped on Mount Kuaiji, and thereupon employed Fan Li and Ji Ran.
translations.idiomatic: In former times, King Goujian of Yue found himself besieged atop Mount Kuaiji. He turned to Fan Li and Ji Ran for counsel.

sentences.zh: 計然曰：「知斗則修備，時用則知物，二者形則萬貨之情可得而觀已。

translations.literal: Ji Ran said: 'If one knows war is coming, one prepares for it; if one knows what the season demands, one understands goods. When both of these take shape, the nature of all ten thousand commodities can be grasped and observed.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Ran said: 'Know that war is coming, and you prepare your defenses. Know what the season demands, and you understand the nature of goods. When both of these are perceived, the workings of all ten thousand commodities can be grasped.

sentences.zh: 故歲在金，穰；

translations.literal: Therefore when the year corresponds to Metal, there is abundance;
translations.idiomatic: When the year corresponds to Metal, there is abundance;

sentences.zh: 水，毀；

translations.literal: when to Water, there is ruin;
translations.idiomatic: when to Water, ruin;

sentences.zh: 木，饑；

translations.literal: when to Wood, there is famine;
translations.idiomatic: when to Wood, famine;

sentences.zh: 火，旱。

translations.literal: when to Fire, there is drought.
translations.idiomatic: and when to Fire, drought.

sentences.zh: 旱則資舟，水則資車，物之理也。

translations.literal: In drought, invest in boats; in flood, invest in carts — this is the principle of commodities.
translations.idiomatic: In times of drought, invest in boats; in times of flood, invest in carts. Such is the principle governing commodities.

sentences.zh: 六歲穰，六歲旱，十二歲一大饑。

translations.literal: Six years of plenty, six years of drought, and once every twelve years a great famine.
translations.idiomatic: Every six years brings abundance, every six years drought, and once in twelve years a great famine.

sentences.zh: 夫糶，二十病農，九十病末。

translations.literal: Now, regarding grain prices: at twenty cash, the farmer suffers; at ninety cash, the merchant suffers.
translations.idiomatic: As for grain prices: when they fall to twenty cash, the farmers suffer; when they rise to ninety, the merchants suffer.

sentences.zh: 末病則財不出，農病則草不辟矣。

translations.literal: When merchants suffer, wealth does not circulate; when farmers suffer, the land is not cleared.
translations.idiomatic: When the merchants suffer, wealth ceases to circulate. When the farmers suffer, the land is left uncultivated.

sentences.zh: 上不過八十，下不減三十，則農末俱利，平糶齊物，關市不乏，治國之道也。

translations.literal: If the price does not exceed eighty above and does not fall below thirty below, then both farmers and merchants profit, grain prices are equalized and goods are balanced, markets and border passes lack nothing — this is the way to govern a state.
translations.idiomatic: If the price never exceeds eighty nor falls below thirty, then both farmer and merchant profit together. Grain prices are stabilized, goods are balanced, and the markets and border passes never run short. This is the way to govern a state.

sentences.zh: 積著之理，務完物，無息幣。

translations.literal: The principle of accumulating and storing: strive to keep goods intact, and do not let money sit idle.
translations.idiomatic: The principle of accumulation and storage: strive to keep goods in perfect condition, and never let money sit idle.

sentences.zh: 以物相貿易，腐敗而食之貨勿留，無敢居貴。

translations.literal: In bartering goods, do not hold on to perishable foodstuffs, and do not dare hoard in hopes of high prices.
translations.idiomatic: When trading goods, do not hold on to perishable commodities, and never dare to hoard in hopes of driving up the price.

sentences.zh: 論其有餘不足，則知貴賤。

translations.literal: By examining what is in surplus and what is in shortage, one can know what is dear and what is cheap.
translations.idiomatic: Examine what is in surplus and what is in shortage, and you will know what is dear and what is cheap.

sentences.zh: 貴上極則反賤，賤下極則反貴。

translations.literal: When prices rise to their extreme, they will reverse and become cheap; when they fall to their extreme, they will reverse and become dear.
translations.idiomatic: When prices rise to the extreme, they will reverse and fall. When they fall to the extreme, they will reverse and rise.

sentences.zh: 貴出如糞土，賤取如珠玉。

translations.literal: Sell when prices are high as though casting away dirt and dung; buy when prices are low as though acquiring pearls and jade.
translations.idiomatic: When prices are high, sell as though throwing away dirt. When prices are low, buy as though acquiring pearls and jade.

sentences.zh: 財幣欲其行如流水。」

translations.literal: Wealth and currency should be kept flowing like running water.'
translations.idiomatic: Wealth and currency must flow like running water.'

sentences.zh: 修之十年，國富，厚賂戰士，士赴矢石，如渴得飲，遂報彊吳，觀兵中國，稱號「五霸」。

translations.literal: He cultivated these principles for ten years, and the state grew rich. He lavished rewards on his warriors, and the soldiers rushed into arrows and stones as eagerly as a thirsty man reaching for drink. He avenged himself upon mighty Wu, paraded his armies before the Central States, and was numbered among the 'Five Hegemons.'
translations.idiomatic: Goujian followed these principles for ten years, and his state grew rich. He lavished rewards upon his warriors, and the soldiers charged into arrows and stones as eagerly as a thirsty man reaching for drink. He avenged himself upon mighty Wu, paraded his armies before the Central States, and earned the title of one of the Five Hegemons.

sentences.zh: 范蠡既雪會稽之恥，乃喟然而嘆曰：「計然之策七，越用其五而得意。

translations.literal: After Fan Li had wiped away the shame of Kuaiji, he sighed deeply and said: 'Of Ji Ran's seven strategies, Yue employed five and achieved its aims.
translations.idiomatic: After Fan Li had washed away the humiliation of Kuaiji, he heaved a deep sigh and said: 'Of Ji Ran's seven strategies, Yue employed only five and achieved everything it sought.

sentences.zh: 既已施於國，吾欲用之家。」

translations.literal: Since these have already been applied to the state, I wish to apply them to my own household.'
translations.idiomatic: Now that they have been applied to the state, I intend to apply them to my own household.'

sentences.zh: 乃乘扁舟浮於江湖，變名易姓，適齊為鴟夷子皮，之陶為朱公。

translations.literal: He then boarded a small boat and floated upon the rivers and lakes, changed his name and surname, went to Qi where he became known as Chiyi Zipi, and moved to Tao where he became known as Tao Zhugong.
translations.idiomatic: He boarded a small boat and set out across the rivers and lakes, changing his name and surname. In Qi he was known as Chiyi Zipi; when he moved to Tao, he took the name Tao Zhugong.

sentences.zh: 朱公以為陶天下之中，諸侯四通，貨物所交易也。

translations.literal: Zhugong considered Tao to be the center of the realm, with routes to the feudal lords open in all four directions — a place where goods were traded.
translations.idiomatic: Zhugong judged Tao to be the center of the realm, with roads leading to the feudal lords in all four directions — a natural hub for the exchange of goods.

sentences.zh: 乃治產積居。

translations.literal: He then managed his property and accumulated stores.
translations.idiomatic: He set about managing his estates and accumulating goods.

sentences.zh: 與時逐而不責於人。

translations.literal: He pursued opportunities as they arose and did not impose demands on others.
translations.idiomatic: He pursued the opportunities of the moment without making demands on others.

sentences.zh: 故善治生者，能擇人而任時。

translations.literal: Therefore one skilled at managing livelihood can choose the right people and seize the right moment.
translations.idiomatic: Thus the man who excels at managing his livelihood knows how to choose the right people and seize the right moment.

sentences.zh: 十九年之中三致千金，再分散與貧交疏昆弟。

translations.literal: Within nineteen years he amassed a thousand in gold three times, and twice distributed it to poor friends and distant kinsmen.
translations.idiomatic: Within nineteen years he amassed a fortune of a thousand in gold three times over, and twice gave it all away to poor friends and distant kinsmen.

sentences.zh: 此所謂富好行其德者也。

translations.literal: This is what is called being wealthy and delighting in practicing virtue.
translations.idiomatic: This is what it means to be wealthy and to delight in practicing virtue.

sentences.zh: 後年衰老而聽子孫，子孫修業而息之，遂至巨萬。

translations.literal: In his later years, growing old, he entrusted affairs to his sons and grandsons; they carried on the business and grew it, eventually reaching enormous wealth.
translations.idiomatic: In his later years, as age overtook him, he handed the business to his sons and grandsons. They carried on and expanded it, eventually amassing an enormous fortune.

sentences.zh: 故言富者皆稱陶朱公。

translations.literal: Therefore when people speak of wealth, they all cite Tao Zhugong.
translations.idiomatic: And so whenever people speak of great wealth, they invoke the name of Tao Zhugong.

sentences.zh: 子贛既學於仲尼，退而仕於衛，廢著鬻財於曹、魯之閒，七十子之徒，賜最為饒益。

translations.literal: Zigong, after studying under Confucius, withdrew and served in Wei, and traded goods and amassed wealth between Cao and Lu. Among the seventy disciples, Zigong was the most prosperous.
translations.idiomatic: Zigong studied under Confucius, then withdrew to serve in Wei, trading goods and accumulating wealth between Cao and Lu. Of all the seventy disciples, Zigong was by far the most prosperous.

sentences.zh: 原憲不厭糟糠，匿於窮巷。

translations.literal: Yuan Xian did not even have his fill of chaff and dregs, and hid in an impoverished alley.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Xian could not even fill his belly with chaff and dregs, and lived hidden away in a destitute alley.

sentences.zh: 子貢結駟連騎，束帛之幣以聘享諸侯，所至，國君無不分庭與之抗禮。

translations.literal: Zigong rode in a four-horse carriage followed by a retinue of riders, bearing bundles of silk as gifts to visit and feast the feudal lords; wherever he went, no ruler failed to divide the court with him and treat him as an equal.
translations.idiomatic: Zigong rode in a four-horse carriage with a retinue of mounted escorts, bearing bundles of silk as gifts to visit and feast the feudal lords. Wherever he went, rulers divided the court and received him with the courtesy due an equal.

sentences.zh: 夫使孔子名布揚於天下者，子貢先後之也。

translations.literal: Now, the one who caused the name of Confucius to be spread and celebrated throughout the realm was Zigong, supporting him from first to last.
translations.idiomatic: It was Zigong, working tirelessly before and after, who caused the name of Confucius to be spread and celebrated throughout the realm.

sentences.zh: 此所謂得埶而益彰者乎？

translations.literal: Is this not what is called gaining influence and thereby growing all the more illustrious?
translations.idiomatic: Is this not a case of gaining influence and thereby shining all the more brightly?

sentences.zh: 白圭，周人也。

translations.literal: Bai Gui was a man of Zhou.
translations.idiomatic: Bai Gui was a man from the state of Zhou.

sentences.zh: 當魏文侯時，李克務盡地力，而白圭樂觀時變，故人棄我取，人取我與。

translations.literal: In the time of Marquis Wen of Wei, Li Ke devoted himself to exhausting the productive capacity of the land, while Bai Gui delighted in observing the changes of the seasons; therefore, what others discard, I take; what others take, I give.
translations.idiomatic: In the time of Marquis Wen of Wei, Li Ke devoted himself to maximizing the yield of the land, while Bai Gui took delight in reading the shifts of the seasons. His principle was: what others discard, I collect; what others seek, I release.

sentences.zh: 夫歲孰取穀，予之絲漆；

translations.literal: When the harvest year is good, buy grain and give silk and lacquer in exchange;
translations.idiomatic: In a good harvest year, buy grain and offer silk and lacquer in exchange;

sentences.zh: 繭出取帛絮，予之食。

translations.literal: when cocoons come out, buy silk floss and cloth, and give food in exchange.
translations.idiomatic: when the cocoons come in, buy silk floss and cloth, and offer food in exchange.

sentences.zh: 太陰在卯，穰；

translations.literal: When Jupiter is in the mao position, there is abundance;
translations.idiomatic: When Jupiter is in the mao position, there is abundance;

sentences.zh: 明歲衰惡。

translations.literal: the following year there will be decline and hardship.
translations.idiomatic: the following year will bring decline and hardship.

sentences.zh: 至午，旱；

translations.literal: When it reaches the wu position, drought;
translations.idiomatic: When Jupiter reaches the wu position, there is drought;

sentences.zh: 明歲美。

translations.literal: the following year will be good.
translations.idiomatic: the following year will be a good one.

sentences.zh: 至酉，穰；

translations.literal: When it reaches the you position, abundance;
translations.idiomatic: When it reaches the you position, there is abundance;

sentences.zh: 明歲衰惡。

translations.literal: the following year there will be decline and hardship.
translations.idiomatic: the following year will bring decline and hardship.

sentences.zh: 至子，大旱；

translations.literal: When it reaches the zi position, severe drought;
translations.idiomatic: When it reaches the zi position, there is severe drought;

sentences.zh: 明歲美，有水。

translations.literal: the following year will be good, with flooding.
translations.idiomatic: the following year will be a good one, though with flooding.

sentences.zh: 至卯，積著率歲倍。

translations.literal: When it returns to the mao position, accumulated stores generally double each year.
translations.idiomatic: By the time it returns to the mao position, accumulated stores have generally doubled each year.

sentences.zh: 欲長錢，取下穀；

translations.literal: If you wish to increase your money, buy low-grade grain;
translations.idiomatic: To increase your cash, buy low-grade grain;

sentences.zh: 長石斗，取上種。

translations.literal: if you wish to increase your bushels and pecks, buy top-grade seed.
translations.idiomatic: to increase your stores of grain by the bushel, buy top-grade seed.

sentences.zh: 能薄飲食，忍嗜欲，節衣服，與用事僮仆同苦樂，趨時若猛獸摯鳥之發。

translations.literal: He could eat sparingly, suppress his desires, economize on clothing, share both hardships and pleasures with his working servants, and seize the moment as fiercely as a predatory beast or a striking bird of prey.
translations.idiomatic: He ate sparingly, suppressed his desires, and economized on clothing. He shared both hardship and ease with his servants, and when opportunity arose, he seized it with the ferocity of a predatory beast or a raptor striking its prey.

sentences.zh: 故曰：「吾治生產，猶伊尹、呂尚之謀，孫吳用兵，商鞅行法是也。

translations.literal: Therefore he said: 'My management of livelihood and production is like the stratagems of Yi Yin and Lü Shang, the deployment of troops by Sun and Wu, and the implementation of law by Shang Yang.
translations.idiomatic: He declared: 'My approach to managing commerce and production is comparable to the stratagems of Yi Yin and Lü Shang, the military art of Sunzi and Wu Qi, and Shang Yang's implementation of the law.

sentences.zh: 是故其智不足與權變，勇不足以決斷，仁不能以取予，彊不能有所守，雖欲學吾術，終不告之矣。」

translations.literal: Therefore, if one's intelligence is insufficient for weighing circumstances and adapting, one's courage insufficient for making decisions, one's benevolence insufficient for knowing when to give and take, and one's firmness insufficient for holding one's ground — though one may wish to learn my methods, I will never divulge them.'
translations.idiomatic: If a man's intelligence is insufficient for weighing circumstances and adapting, if his courage is insufficient for decisive action, if his benevolence is insufficient for knowing when to give and when to take, and if his firmness is insufficient for holding his ground — though he may wish to learn my methods, I will never teach him.'

sentences.zh: 蓋天下言治生祖白圭。

translations.literal: Thus all under heaven who speak of managing livelihood regard Bai Gui as their founder.
translations.idiomatic: And so throughout the realm, all who speak of managing commerce regard Bai Gui as their founder.

sentences.zh: 白圭其有所試矣，能試有所長，非茍而已也。

translations.literal: Bai Gui had indeed put his principles to the test; his tested methods had genuine strengths — it was not mere idle talk.
translations.idiomatic: Bai Gui had truly put his principles to the test. His proven methods had real substance — they were no idle boast.

sentences.zh: 猗頓用盬鹽起。

translations.literal: Yi Dun rose through the trade of Anyi salt.
translations.idiomatic: Yi Dun built his fortune through the salt trade of Anyi.

sentences.zh: 而邯鄲郭縱以鐵冶成業，與王者埒富。

translations.literal: And Guo Zong of Handan established his enterprise through iron smelting, amassing wealth rivaling that of kings.
translations.idiomatic: Guo Zong of Handan built his enterprise through iron smelting, amassing wealth to rival that of kings.

sentences.zh: 烏氏倮牧，及眾，斥賣，求奇繒物，閒獻遺戎王。

translations.literal: Luo of the Wushi clan herded livestock; when his herds grew numerous, he sold them off, sought rare silks and unusual goods, and from time to time presented gifts to the Rong king.
translations.idiomatic: Luo of the Wushi clan began as a herdsman. When his flocks grew large, he sold them off, invested in rare silks and exotic goods, and periodically presented gifts to the Rong king.

sentences.zh: 戎王什倍其償，與之畜，畜至用谷量馬牛。

translations.literal: The Rong king repaid him tenfold, giving him livestock; his livestock grew until his horses and cattle were measured by the valley.
translations.idiomatic: The Rong king repaid him tenfold in livestock. His herds grew so vast that his horses and cattle were counted by the valley.

sentences.zh: 秦始皇帝令倮比封君，以時與列臣朝請。

translations.literal: The First Emperor of Qin ordered that Luo be treated as the equal of an enfeoffed lord, and at the proper times he attended court audiences alongside the ranked ministers.
translations.idiomatic: The First Emperor of Qin decreed that Luo be accorded the status of an enfeoffed lord, attending court audiences alongside the ranked ministers at the proper times.

sentences.zh: 而巴[蜀]寡婦清，其先得丹穴，而擅其利數世，家亦不訾。

translations.literal: And the Widow Qing of Ba, whose ancestors had discovered cinnabar mines and monopolized their profits for several generations — her household's wealth was incalculable.
translations.idiomatic: The Widow Qing of Ba descended from a family that had discovered cinnabar mines and monopolized their profits for generations. Her household's wealth was beyond reckoning.

sentences.zh: 清，寡婦也，能守其業，用財自衛，不見侵犯。

translations.literal: Qing was a widow who was able to maintain her family's enterprise, using her wealth to protect herself, and was never violated or harmed.
translations.idiomatic: Though a widow, Qing maintained her family's enterprise and used her wealth to defend herself, so that no one dared encroach upon her.

sentences.zh: 秦皇帝以為貞婦而客之，為筑女懷清臺。

translations.literal: The First Emperor of Qin considered her a virtuous woman and treated her as an honored guest, building the 'Terrace of the Lady Huaiqing' in her honor.
translations.idiomatic: The First Emperor deemed her a virtuous woman and received her as a guest of honor, building the Terrace of Lady Huaiqing in her name.

sentences.zh: 夫倮鄙人牧長，清窮鄉寡婦，禮抗萬乘，名顯天下，豈非以富邪？

translations.literal: Now Luo was a herdsman of the frontier, and Qing a village widow from a remote place, yet in courtesy they were treated as equals of rulers of ten thousand chariots, and their names were known throughout the realm — was this not because of wealth?
translations.idiomatic: Luo was but a herdsman of the frontier, and Qing a widow from a remote village, yet both were received with the courtesy due to rulers of ten thousand chariots, and their names resounded throughout the realm. Was this not because of their wealth?

sentences.zh: 漢興，海內為一，開關梁，弛山澤之禁，是以富商大賈周流天下，交易之物莫不通，得其所欲，而徙豪傑諸侯彊族於京師。

translations.literal: When the Han arose, the empire was unified; the passes and bridges were opened, and the prohibitions on mountains and marshes were relaxed. Thus wealthy merchants and great traders circulated throughout the realm, and no good was barred from trade; each obtained what he desired. The powerful men, feudal families, and mighty clans were relocated to the capital.
translations.idiomatic: When the Han dynasty arose and the empire was unified, the border passes and bridges were thrown open and the restrictions on the mountains and marshlands relaxed. Great merchants and wealthy traders ranged freely throughout the realm, no commodity was barred from exchange, and each man obtained what he desired. The powerful families and mighty clans of the former feudal states were relocated to the capital.

sentences.zh: 關中自汧、雍以東至河、華，膏壤沃野千里，自虞夏之貢以為上田，而公劉適邠，大王、王季在岐，文王作豐，武王治鎬，故其民猶有先王之遺風，好稼穡，殖五穀，地重，重為邪。

translations.literal: Within the passes, from Qian and Yong eastward to the Yellow River and Mount Hua, there stretches a thousand li of rich soil and fertile fields, rated as top-grade land since the tribute system of Yu and Xia. Gongliu settled at Bin, the Great King and King Ji resided at Qi, King Wen founded Feng, and King Wu governed from Hao; therefore its people still preserve the legacy of the former kings — they love farming, cultivate the five grains, value the land, and are reluctant to act improperly.
translations.idiomatic: Within the passes, from Qian and Yong eastward to the Yellow River and Mount Hua, there stretches a thousand li of rich soil and fertile plains — land rated as top grade since the tribute system of Yu and Xia. Gongliu settled at Bin, the Great King and King Ji at Qi, King Wen established Feng, and King Wu governed from Hao. The people of this region still preserve the legacy of those former kings: they love farming, cultivate the five grains, prize the land, and are loath to stray from the right path.

sentences.zh: 及秦文、（孝）［德］、繆居雍，隙隴蜀之貨物而多賈。

translations.literal: When Dukes Wen, De, and Mu of Qin resided at Yong, they exploited the gap in goods between Long and Shu, and there were many merchants.
translations.idiomatic: When Dukes Wen, De, and Mu of Qin held court at Yong, they profited from the flow of goods between Long and Shu, and merchants were plentiful.

sentences.zh: 獻（孝）公徙櫟邑，櫟邑北卻戎翟，東通三晉，亦多大賈。

translations.literal: Duke Xian moved to Liyou; Liyou repelled the Rong and Di to the north and connected to the Three Jin to the east, and there too were many great merchants.
translations.idiomatic: Duke Xian relocated to Liyi, which pushed back the Rong and Di to the north and opened trade routes east to the Three Jin states. There too, great merchants abounded.

sentences.zh: （武）［孝］、昭治咸陽，因以漢都，長安諸陵，四方輻湊并至而會，地小人眾，故其民益玩巧而事末也。

translations.literal: Dukes Xiao and Zhao governed from Xianyang, which then became the Han capital; at Chang'an and the various imperial mausoleums, people converged from all four directions like spokes to a hub. The land was small and the population large, so the people grew ever more skilled in cleverness and devoted to commerce.
translations.idiomatic: Dukes Xiao and Zhao governed from Xianyang, which later became the Han capital. At Chang'an and the surrounding imperial mausoleum towns, people converged from every direction like spokes to a hub. The land was cramped and the population dense, so the people grew ever more devoted to clever craftsmanship and commercial pursuits.

sentences.zh: 南則巴蜀。

translations.literal: To the south lies Ba and Shu.
translations.idiomatic: To the south lie Ba and Shu.

sentences.zh: 巴蜀亦沃野，地饒炧、薑、丹沙、石、銅、鐵、竹、木之器。

translations.literal: Ba and Shu also have fertile fields, and the land is rich in lacquer, ginger, cinnabar, stone, copper, iron, and implements of bamboo and wood.
translations.idiomatic: Ba and Shu likewise boast fertile plains. The land is rich in lacquer, ginger, cinnabar, stone, copper, iron, and implements of bamboo and wood.

sentences.zh: 南御滇僰，僰僮。

translations.literal: To the south they border the Dian and Bo peoples; the Bo provide slaves.
translations.idiomatic: To the south, they border the Dian and Bo peoples, who supply slaves.

sentences.zh: 西近邛笮，笮馬、旄牛。

translations.literal: To the west they are close to the Qiong and Zuo peoples, who provide Zuo horses and yaks.
translations.idiomatic: To the west, they adjoin the Qiong and Zuo peoples, who supply fine horses and yaks.

sentences.zh: 然四塞，棧道千里，無所不通，唯褒斜綰轂其口，以所多易所鮮。

translations.literal: Though walled in on four sides, the plank roads extend a thousand li and reach everywhere; only the Bao and Xie passes control the entrance, where they exchange what they have in abundance for what they lack.
translations.idiomatic: Though hemmed in on all four sides, the plank roads extend a thousand li and connect to everywhere. Only the Bao and Xie passes serve as the gateway, where the people exchange what they have in abundance for what they lack.

sentences.zh: 天水、隴西、北地、上郡與關中同俗，然西有羌中之利，北有戎翟之畜，畜牧為天下饒。

translations.literal: Tianshui, Longxi, Beidi, and Shangjun share the same customs as the region within the passes, yet to the west they enjoy the profits of the Qiang lands, and to the north the livestock of the Rong and Di; in animal husbandry they are the richest in the realm.
translations.idiomatic: Tianshui, Longxi, Beidi, and Shangjun share the customs of the region within the passes, but to the west they profit from the Qiang lands, and to the north from the livestock of the Rong and Di. In animal husbandry, they are the richest in the realm.

sentences.zh: 然地亦窮險，唯京師要其道。

translations.literal: Yet the terrain is also remote and rugged; only the capital commands the routes to them.
translations.idiomatic: Yet the terrain is remote and rugged, and only the capital commands the key routes connecting them.

sentences.zh: 故關中之地，於天下三分之一，而人眾不過什三；

translations.literal: Therefore the land within the passes constitutes one-third of the realm, and its population does not exceed three-tenths;
translations.idiomatic: The land within the passes constitutes roughly a third of the realm, and its population amounts to no more than three-tenths of the whole;

sentences.zh: 然量其富，什居其六。

translations.literal: yet measured by its wealth, it accounts for six-tenths.
translations.idiomatic: yet measured by wealth, it accounts for fully six-tenths of the total.

sentences.zh: 昔唐人都河東，殷人都河內，周人都河南。

translations.literal: In ancient times, the people of Tang had their capital east of the Yellow River, the Yin had theirs within the bend of the river, and the Zhou had theirs south of the river.
translations.idiomatic: In ancient times, the Tang dynasty established its capital east of the Yellow River, the Yin within the river's bend, and the Zhou to the river's south.

sentences.zh: 夫三河在天下之中，若鼎足，王者所更居也，建國各數百千歲，土地小狹，民人眾，都國諸侯所聚會，故其俗纖儉習事。

translations.literal: Now the Three River region lies at the center of the realm, like the three legs of a tripod — it is the place where kings have taken turns residing, and states have been established there for hundreds or thousands of years. The territory is small and cramped, the population dense, and the capitals and feudal courts all gather there; therefore its customs are refined, frugal, and practiced in affairs.
translations.idiomatic: The Three River region sits at the center of the realm, like the three legs of a tripod. Successive kings have made their residence here, and states have endured for hundreds or even thousands of years. The territory is small and cramped, the population dense, and the courts of capitals and feudal lords all converge here. For this reason, the customs of the people are refined, frugal, and practiced in the ways of business.

sentences.zh: 楊、平陽陳西賈秦、翟，北賈種、代。

translations.literal: Yang and Pingyang trade westward with Qin and the Di, and northward with Zhong and Dai.
translations.idiomatic: The people of Yang and Pingyang trade westward with Qin and the Di, and northward with Zhong and Dai.

sentences.zh: 種、代，石北也，地邊胡，數被寇。

translations.literal: Zhong and Dai lie north of the stony land, bordering the Hu peoples, and are frequently raided.
translations.idiomatic: Zhong and Dai lie to the north of the stony lands, bordering the Hu peoples, and suffer frequent raids.

sentences.zh: 人民矜懻忮，好氣，任俠為姦，不事農商。

translations.literal: The people are proud, quarrelsome, and contentious; they love spirit, serve as knights-errant and engage in lawlessness, and do not devote themselves to farming or commerce.
translations.idiomatic: The people are proud, quarrelsome, and hot-tempered. They relish daring exploits, act as knights-errant, and engage in lawless behavior, spurning both farming and commerce.

sentences.zh: 然迫近北夷，師旅亟往，中國委輸時有奇羨。

translations.literal: Yet being pressed close to the northern barbarians, armies frequently march through, and the Central States supply and transport goods, sometimes leaving surpluses behind.
translations.idiomatic: Yet because they are close to the northern barbarians, armies frequently march through, and the Central States' supply convoys sometimes leave behind unexpected surpluses.

sentences.zh: 其民羯羠不均，自全晉之時固已患其僄悍，而武靈王益厲之，其謠俗猶有趙之風也。

translations.literal: Their people are coarse and uneven in character; since the time of united Jin, their violence and fierce temper were already a concern, and King Wuling encouraged it further. Their songs and customs still retain the spirit of Zhao.
translations.idiomatic: The people are rough and uneven in temperament. Even in the days of united Jin, their fierce and violent spirit was cause for concern, and King Wuling only encouraged it further. Their songs and customs still bear the stamp of Zhao.

sentences.zh: 故楊、平陽陳掾其閒，得所欲。

translations.literal: Therefore the merchants of Yang and Pingyang ply their trade among them and obtain what they desire.
translations.idiomatic: And so the merchants of Yang and Pingyang ply their trade among them and obtain whatever they desire.

sentences.zh: 溫、軹西賈上黨，北賈趙、中山。

translations.literal: Wen and Zhi trade westward with Shangdang and northward with Zhao and Zhongshan.
translations.idiomatic: The people of Wen and Zhi trade westward with Shangdang and northward with Zhao and Zhongshan.

sentences.zh: 中山地薄人眾，猶有沙丘紂淫地餘民，民俗懁急，仰機利而食。

translations.literal: Zhongshan has thin soil and a large population, and still harbors remnants of the people from Shaqiu, the place of Zhou's debauchery. The popular customs are crafty and hasty, and they rely on cunning and profit-seeking for their livelihood.
translations.idiomatic: Zhongshan has thin soil and a dense population, and still harbors remnant populations from Shaqiu, the site of Zhou of Shang's debaucheries. The customs are crafty and impetuous, and the people depend on cunning and profit-seeking for their livelihood.

sentences.zh: 丈夫相聚游戲，悲歌慨，起則相隨椎剽，休則掘冢作巧姦冶，多美物，為倡優。

translations.literal: The men gather together to amuse themselves, singing sorrowful songs with emotion; when active they go about in gangs committing robbery and murder; when idle they dig up tombs and produce cunning counterfeit goods, fine objects, and serve as performers and entertainers.
translations.idiomatic: The men gather to amuse themselves, singing mournful ballads with gusto. When stirred to action, they band together in gangs to rob and kill; when at rest, they dig up tombs, craft cunning counterfeits and fine trinkets, and work as performers and entertainers.

sentences.zh: 女子則鼓鳴瑟，跕屣，游媚貴富，入後宮，遍諸侯。

translations.literal: The women play drums and pluck the zither, trip along in slippers, and rove about charming the rich and powerful; they enter rear palaces and spread throughout the feudal courts.
translations.idiomatic: The women play drums and pluck the zither, trip along in fine slippers, and wander about charming the rich and powerful. They enter the inner palaces and find their way into the courts of feudal lords throughout the realm.

sentences.zh: 然邯鄲亦漳、河之閒一都會也。

translations.literal: Now Handan is also a great commercial city between the Zhang River and the Yellow River.
translations.idiomatic: Handan, too, is a great commercial hub situated between the Zhang River and the Yellow River.

sentences.zh: 北通燕、涿，南有鄭、衛。

translations.literal: To the north it connects with Yan and Zhuo; to the south lie Zheng and Wei.
translations.idiomatic: To the north it connects with Yan and Zhuo; to the south lie Zheng and Wei.

sentences.zh: 鄭、衛俗與趙相類，然近梁、魯，微重而矜節。

translations.literal: The customs of Zheng and Wei are similar to those of Zhao, yet being close to Liang and Lu, they are somewhat more serious and pride themselves on propriety.
translations.idiomatic: The customs of Zheng and Wei resemble those of Zhao, yet their proximity to Liang and Lu lends them a somewhat weightier character and a greater pride in propriety.

sentences.zh: 濮上之邑徙野王，野王好氣任俠，衛之風也。

translations.literal: The towns along the Pu River were relocated to Yewang; Yewang loves spirited daring and knight-errantry — this is the spirit of Wei.
translations.idiomatic: The towns along the Pu River were relocated to Yewang. The people of Yewang love spirited daring and knight-errantry — the very spirit of Wei.

sentences.zh: 夫燕亦勃、碣之閒一都會也。

translations.literal: Now Yan is also a great commercial city between the Gulf of Bo and Mount Jie.
translations.idiomatic: Yan, too, is a great commercial hub situated between the Gulf of Bohai and Mount Jieshi.

sentences.zh: 南通齊、趙，東北邊胡。

translations.literal: To the south it connects with Qi and Zhao; to the northeast it borders the Hu peoples.
translations.idiomatic: To the south it connects with Qi and Zhao; to the northeast it borders the Hu peoples.

sentences.zh: 上谷至遼東，地踔遠，人民希，數被寇，大與趙、代俗相類，而民雕捍少慮，有魚鹽棗栗之饒。

translations.literal: From Shanggu to Liaodong, the land is remote and far-flung, the population sparse, and frequently raided. The customs are broadly similar to those of Zhao and Dai, and the people are tough, fierce, and heedless. The region has an abundance of fish, salt, dates, and chestnuts.
translations.idiomatic: From Shanggu to Liaodong, the territory stretches far into the distance, the population is sparse, and raids are frequent. The customs broadly resemble those of Zhao and Dai — the people are tough, fierce, and reckless. The region abounds in fish, salt, dates, and chestnuts.

sentences.zh: 北鄰烏桓、夫餘，東綰穢貉、朝鮮、真番之利。

translations.literal: To the north it borders the Wuhuan and Fuyu peoples; to the east it controls the profits of the Huimo, Joseon, and Zhenpan.
translations.idiomatic: To the north it borders the Wuhuan and Fuyu; to the east it commands the profits of the Huimo, Joseon, and Zhenpan.

sentences.zh: 洛陽東賈齊、魯，南賈梁、楚。

translations.literal: Luoyang trades eastward with Qi and Lu, and southward with Liang and Chu.
translations.idiomatic: Luoyang trades eastward with Qi and Lu, and southward with Liang and Chu.

sentences.zh: 故泰山之陽則魯，其陰則齊。

translations.literal: The southern slope of Mount Tai belongs to Lu; its northern slope belongs to Qi.
translations.idiomatic: The southern slope of Mount Tai belongs to Lu, and the northern slope to Qi.

sentences.zh: 齊帶山海，膏壤千里，宜桑麻，人民多文綵布帛魚鹽。

translations.literal: Qi is girded by mountains and sea, with a thousand li of rich soil; it is suited for mulberry and hemp, and its people have an abundance of patterned colored cloth, silk fabrics, fish, and salt.
translations.idiomatic: Qi is girded by mountains and sea, with a thousand li of fertile soil ideal for mulberry and hemp. Its people produce a wealth of patterned cloth, silk, fish, and salt.

sentences.zh: 臨菑亦海岱之閒一都會也。

translations.literal: Linzi is also a great commercial city between the sea and Mount Tai.
translations.idiomatic: Linzi, too, is a great commercial hub situated between the sea and Mount Tai.

sentences.zh: 其俗寬緩闊達，而足智，好議論，地重，難動搖，怯於眾鬬，勇於持刺，故多劫人者，大國之風也。

translations.literal: Its customs are broad-minded, easygoing, and open; the people are full of cleverness, love debate, value the land, and are hard to stir up. They are timid in mass combat but bold at assassination, and therefore there are many who waylay others — such is the character of a great state.
translations.idiomatic: The customs are broad-minded, leisurely, and open. The people are clever, love debate, prize their land, and are difficult to rouse. They are timid in pitched battle but bold when it comes to assassination, and so there are many who resort to robbery. Such is the character of a great state.

sentences.zh: 其中具五民。

translations.literal: Its population encompasses all five classes of people.
translations.idiomatic: Its population encompasses all five classes of people.

sentences.zh: 而鄒、魯濱洙、泗，猶有周公遺風，俗好儒，備於禮，故其民齪齪。

translations.literal: Zou and Lu, situated along the Zhu and Si rivers, still preserve the legacy of the Duke of Zhou; their customs favor Confucian learning and are thorough in ritual, so the people are careful and diligent.
translations.idiomatic: Zou and Lu, along the banks of the Zhu and Si rivers, still preserve the legacy of the Duke of Zhou. Their customs favor Confucian learning and are meticulous in matters of ritual, so the people are careful and diligent.

sentences.zh: 頗有桑麻之業，無林澤之饒。

translations.literal: They have a fair amount of mulberry and hemp production, but lack the abundance of forests and marshes.
translations.idiomatic: They engage in the cultivation of mulberry and hemp, but lack the riches of forests and marshlands.

sentences.zh: 地小人眾，儉嗇，畏罪遠邪。

translations.literal: The land is small and the population large; they are frugal and thrifty, fear punishment, and shun wrongdoing.
translations.idiomatic: The land is small and the population dense. The people are frugal and thrifty, fearful of punishment, and keep their distance from wrongdoing.

sentences.zh: 及其衰，好賈趨利，甚於周人。

translations.literal: But when their fortunes declined, they took to commerce and pursued profit even more avidly than the people of Zhou.
translations.idiomatic: But when their fortunes declined, they threw themselves into commerce and the pursuit of profit with an eagerness surpassing even that of the Zhou people.

sentences.zh: 夫自鴻溝以東，芒、碭以北，屬巨野，此梁、宋也。

translations.literal: East of the Hong Canal, north of Mang and Dang, reaching to Juye — this is the region of Liang and Song.
translations.idiomatic: East of the Hong Canal, north of Mang and Dang, stretching to the marshes of Juye — this is the territory of Liang and Song.

sentences.zh: 陶、睢陽亦一都會也。

translations.literal: Tao and Suiyang are also great commercial cities.
translations.idiomatic: Tao and Suiyang are likewise great commercial hubs.

sentences.zh: 昔堯作（游）［於］成陽，舜漁於雷澤，湯止于亳。

translations.literal: In ancient times, Yao resided at Chengyang, Shun fished at Lei Marsh, and Tang settled at Bo.
translations.idiomatic: In ancient times, Yao held court at Chengyang, Shun fished at Lei Marsh, and Tang established himself at Bo.

sentences.zh: 其俗猶有先王遺風，重厚多君子，好稼穡，雖無山川之饒，能惡衣食，致其蓄藏。

translations.literal: Its customs still preserve the legacy of the former kings: the people are serious and substantial, with many gentlemen among them. They love farming, and though they lack the riches of mountains and rivers, they are willing to wear coarse clothes and eat plain food in order to build up their stores.
translations.idiomatic: The customs still preserve the legacy of the former kings. The people are serious and upright, with many gentlemen among them. They love farming, and though the land lacks the riches of mountains and rivers, they willingly wear coarse clothing and eat plain food in order to build up their stores.

sentences.zh: 越、楚則有三俗。

translations.literal: Yue and Chu have three distinct sets of customs.
translations.idiomatic: The regions of Yue and Chu can be divided into three distinct zones of custom.

sentences.zh: 夫自淮北沛、陳、汝南、南郡，此西楚也。

translations.literal: From Pei, Chen, Runan, and Nanjun north of the Huai River — this is Western Chu.
translations.idiomatic: Pei, Chen, Runan, and Nanjun, north of the Huai — this is the region of Western Chu.

sentences.zh: 其俗剽輕，易發怒，地薄，寡於積聚。

translations.literal: Its customs are swift and impetuous; the people are quick to anger. The land is thin and they accumulate little.
translations.idiomatic: Its customs are reckless and impetuous, the people quick to anger. The soil is thin, and they accumulate little.

sentences.zh: 江陵故郢都，西通巫、巴，東有雲夢之饒。

translations.literal: Jiangling is the former Ying capital; to the west it connects with Wu and Ba, and to the east it has the riches of the Yunmeng marshes.
translations.idiomatic: Jiangling, the former Ying capital, connects westward to Wu and Ba, and to the east commands the riches of the Yunmeng marshlands.

sentences.zh: 陳在楚夏之交，通魚鹽之貨，其民多賈。

translations.literal: Chen sits at the intersection of Chu and the Xia region, trading in fish and salt; its people are largely merchants.
translations.idiomatic: Chen sits at the intersection of Chu and the Xia region, channeling the trade in fish and salt. Its people are overwhelmingly merchants.

sentences.zh: 徐、僮、取慮，則清刻，矜己諾。

translations.literal: Xu, Tong, and Qulü are sharp and exacting, proud of keeping their word.
translations.idiomatic: The people of Xu, Tong, and Qulü are sharp and exacting, taking great pride in keeping their word.

sentences.zh: 彭城以東，東海、吳、廣陵，此東楚也。

translations.literal: From Pengcheng eastward to Donghai, Wu, and Guangling — this is Eastern Chu.
translations.idiomatic: From Pengcheng eastward to Donghai, Wu, and Guangling — this is the region of Eastern Chu.

sentences.zh: 其俗類徐、僮。

translations.literal: Its customs resemble those of Xu and Tong.
translations.idiomatic: Its customs resemble those of Xu and Tong.

sentences.zh: 朐、繒以北，俗則齊。

translations.literal: North of Qu and Zeng, the customs are those of Qi.
translations.idiomatic: North of Qu and Zeng, the customs follow those of Qi.

sentences.zh: 浙江南則越。

translations.literal: South of the Zhe River lies Yue.
translations.idiomatic: South of the Zhe River lies Yue.

sentences.zh: 夫吳自闔廬、春申、王濞三人招致天下之喜游子弟，東有海鹽之饒，章山之銅，三江、五湖之利，亦江東一都會也。

translations.literal: Wu, ever since the three men — Helü, Lord Chunshen, and King Bi — attracted wandering youths from throughout the realm, has enjoyed the riches of seaside salt to the east, the copper of Mount Zhang, and the profits of the Three Rivers and Five Lakes; it too is a great commercial hub east of the Yangtze.
translations.idiomatic: Ever since Helü, Lord Chunshen, and King Bi attracted ambitious young men from across the realm, Wu has enjoyed the riches of seaside salt to the east, the copper of Mount Zhang, and the profits of the Three Rivers and Five Lakes. It, too, is a great commercial hub east of the Yangtze.

sentences.zh: 衡山、九江、江南、豫章、長沙，是南楚也，其俗大類西楚。

translations.literal: Hengshan, Jiujiang, Jiangnan, Yuzhang, and Changsha — this is Southern Chu. Its customs are broadly similar to those of Western Chu.
translations.idiomatic: Hengshan, Jiujiang, Jiangnan, Yuzhang, and Changsha — this is the region of Southern Chu. Its customs are broadly similar to those of Western Chu.

sentences.zh: 郢之後徙壽春，亦一都會也。

translations.literal: After the capital was moved from Ying to Shouchun, Shouchun also became a great commercial city.
translations.idiomatic: After the Chu capital was relocated from Ying to Shouchun, Shouchun too became a great commercial hub.

sentences.zh: 而合肥受南北潮，皮革、鮑、木輸會也。

translations.literal: And Hefei receives the tides of commerce from north and south — it is a gathering point for leather hides, salted fish, and timber.
translations.idiomatic: Hefei receives the tides of commerce from both north and south, serving as a gathering point for hides, salted fish, and timber.

sentences.zh: 與閩中、干越雜俗，故南楚好辭，巧說少信。

translations.literal: It mingles with the customs of Min and Gan-Yue, and so Southern Chu is fond of eloquence, skilled in persuasion, but lacking in trustworthiness.
translations.idiomatic: The customs mingle with those of Min and Gan-Yue. For this reason, the people of Southern Chu are fond of fine words and skilled in persuasion, but lacking in trustworthiness.

sentences.zh: 江南卑溼，丈夫早夭。

translations.literal: South of the Yangtze the land is low and damp, and men die young.
translations.idiomatic: South of the Yangtze, the land is low and damp, and men tend to die young.

sentences.zh: 多竹木。

translations.literal: There is an abundance of bamboo and wood.
translations.idiomatic: Bamboo and timber are plentiful.

sentences.zh: 豫章出黃金，長沙出連、錫，然堇堇物之所有，取之不足以更費。

translations.literal: Yuzhang produces gold, and Changsha produces lead and tin, but these resources are so meager that extracting them barely covers the cost.
translations.idiomatic: Yuzhang produces gold, and Changsha yields lead and tin, but the deposits are so meager that the cost of extraction barely justifies the effort.

sentences.zh: 九疑、蒼梧以南至儋耳者，與江南大同俗，而楊越多焉。

translations.literal: From Jiuyi and Cangwu southward to Dan'er, the customs are broadly the same as south of the Yangtze, but with more Yang-Yue elements.
translations.idiomatic: From Jiuyi and Cangwu southward all the way to Dan'er, the customs broadly resemble those south of the Yangtze, but with a stronger Yang-Yue character.

sentences.zh: 番禺亦其一都會也，珠璣、犀、瑁、果、布之湊。

translations.literal: Panyu is also a great commercial city among them — a gathering point for pearls, rhinoceros horn, tortoiseshell, fruit, and cloth.
translations.idiomatic: Panyu, too, is a great commercial hub of the region — a gathering point for pearls, rhinoceros horn, tortoiseshell, fruit, and cloth.

sentences.zh: 潁川、南陽，夏人之居也。

translations.literal: Yingchuan and Nanyang are the homeland of the Xia people.
translations.idiomatic: Yingchuan and Nanyang are the ancestral homeland of the Xia people.

sentences.zh: 夏人政尚忠樸，猶有先王之遺風。

translations.literal: The Xia people in governance esteem loyalty and simplicity, and still preserve the legacy of the former kings.
translations.idiomatic: The Xia people esteem loyalty and simplicity in governance, still preserving the legacy of the former kings.

sentences.zh: 潁川敦願。

translations.literal: The people of Yingchuan are sincere and earnest.
translations.idiomatic: The people of Yingchuan are sincere and earnest.

sentences.zh: 秦末世，遷不軌之民於南陽。

translations.literal: In the last days of Qin, lawless people were relocated to Nanyang.
translations.idiomatic: In the waning days of the Qin, lawless people were relocated to Nanyang.

sentences.zh: 南陽西通武關、鄖關，東南受漢、江、淮。

translations.literal: Nanyang connects westward to Wuguan and Yunguan passes, and to the southeast receives the trade of the Han, Yangtze, and Huai rivers.
translations.idiomatic: Nanyang connects westward to the Wuguan and Yun passes, and to the southeast receives the commerce of the Han, Yangtze, and Huai rivers.

sentences.zh: 宛亦一都會也。

translations.literal: Wan is also a great commercial city.
translations.idiomatic: Wan, too, is a great commercial hub.

sentences.zh: 俗雜好事，業多賈。

translations.literal: Its customs are mixed, and the people love enterprise; commerce predominates among their occupations.
translations.idiomatic: The customs are mixed and the people love enterprise. Commerce predominates among their occupations.

sentences.zh: 其任俠，交通潁川，故至今謂之「夏人」。

translations.literal: Their knight-errantry and connections with Yingchuan explain why to this day they are called 'Xia people.'
translations.idiomatic: Their tradition of knight-errantry and their connections with Yingchuan explain why they are still called 'Xia people' to this day.

sentences.zh: 夫天下物所鮮所多，人民謠俗，山東食海鹽，山西食鹽鹵，領南、沙北固往往出鹽，大體如此矣。

translations.literal: Now, regarding where things are scarce and where they are abundant throughout the realm, and the customs and songs of the people: east of the mountains they eat sea salt, west of the mountains they eat rock salt; south of the mountain passes and north of the deserts, salt is produced here and there. In broad outline, this is how it stands.
translations.idiomatic: As for the distribution of scarcity and abundance throughout the realm, and the customs and character of the people: east of the mountains they eat sea salt, west of the mountains they eat rock salt, and south of the passes and north of the deserts salt is produced in scattered locations. Such, in broad outline, is the picture.

sentences.zh: 總之，楚越之地，地廣人希，飯稻羹魚，或火耕而水耨，果隋蠃蛤，不待賈而足，地埶饒食，無饑饉之患，以故呰窳偷生，無積聚而多貧。

translations.literal: In sum, in the lands of Chu and Yue, the territory is vast and the population sparse. They eat rice and fish soup, practice slash-and-burn agriculture with flooded weeding, and gather fruits, snails, and clams. They have enough without needing trade. The land's natural bounty ensures food is plentiful and there is no fear of famine. For this reason, the people are idle and lazy, living from hand to mouth, with no stores laid up and much poverty.
translations.idiomatic: In sum, the lands of Chu and Yue are vast and thinly populated. The people eat rice and fish soup, practice slash-and-burn farming with flooded weeding, and gather wild fruits, snails, and clams — all sufficient without the need for trade. The land's natural bounty ensures plentiful food with no fear of famine. And so the people live idly, from hand to mouth, laying up no stores, and many remain poor.

sentences.zh: 是故江淮以南，無凍餓之人，亦無千金之家。

translations.literal: Therefore south of the Yangtze and Huai, there are no people who freeze or starve, nor are there any families worth a thousand in gold.
translations.idiomatic: And so south of the Yangtze and the Huai, no one freezes or starves, yet neither are there any families worth a thousand in gold.

sentences.zh: 沂、泗水以北，宜五穀桑麻六畜，地小人眾，數被水旱之害，民好畜藏，故秦、夏、梁、魯好農而重民。

translations.literal: North of the Yi and Si rivers, the land is suitable for the five grains, mulberry, hemp, and the six domestic animals. The territory is small and the population large, frequently afflicted by floods and droughts. The people love to store up savings; therefore the regions of Qin, Xia, Liang, and Lu love agriculture and value their people.
translations.idiomatic: North of the Yi and Si rivers, the land is suited to the five grains, mulberry, hemp, and the six domestic animals. The territory is small, the population large, and floods and droughts are frequent. The people are keen to accumulate savings, and so the regions of Qin, Xia, Liang, and Lu prize agriculture and value the people.

sentences.zh: 三河、宛、陳亦然，加以商賈。

translations.literal: The Three Rivers region, Wan, and Chen are the same, with the addition of commerce.
translations.idiomatic: The Three Rivers, Wan, and Chen follow the same pattern, with commerce added to the mix.

sentences.zh: 齊、趙設智巧，仰機利。

translations.literal: Qi and Zhao employ cleverness and ingenuity, relying on opportunity and profit.
translations.idiomatic: Qi and Zhao employ cleverness and ingenuity, relying on opportunism and profit.

sentences.zh: 燕、代田畜而事蠶。

translations.literal: Yan and Dai farm and raise livestock, and engage in sericulture.
translations.idiomatic: Yan and Dai farm, raise livestock, and practice sericulture.

sentences.zh: 由此觀之，賢人深謀於廊廟，論議朝廷，守信死節隱居巖穴之士設為名高者安歸乎？

translations.literal: Viewed from this perspective — the worthy men who plot deeply in the corridors of the court, who debate in the audience hall, the gentlemen who keep faith unto death, maintain their integrity, and hide in mountain caves, establishing themselves as men of lofty reputation — to what do they all return?
translations.idiomatic: Seen from this vantage point, the worthy men who plot in the corridors of the court, who debate in the audience hall, the gentlemen who keep faith unto death, uphold their integrity, and hide away in mountain caves, men who cultivate lofty reputations — to what do they all return in the end?

sentences.zh: 歸於富厚也。

translations.literal: They return to wealth and abundance.
translations.idiomatic: They return, in the end, to wealth and abundance.

sentences.zh: 是以廉吏久，久更富，廉賈歸富。

translations.literal: Therefore an honest official who serves long enough eventually grows rich, and an honest merchant ultimately becomes wealthy.
translations.idiomatic: And so an honest official who serves long enough eventually grows rich, and an honest merchant in the end grows wealthy.

sentences.zh: 富者，人之情性，所不學而俱欲者也。

translations.literal: Wealth is something rooted in human nature — something all desire without needing to be taught.
translations.idiomatic: The desire for wealth is rooted in human nature. It is something all men crave without ever having to be taught.

sentences.zh: 故壯士在軍，攻城先登，陷陣卻敵，斬將搴旗，前蒙矢石，不避湯火之難者，為重賞使也。

translations.literal: Therefore brave soldiers in the army who are the first to scale the walls in an assault, who break through enemy lines, cut down generals and seize banners, who rush forward into arrows and stones and do not shrink from boiling water or raging fire — they do so because they are driven by the promise of lavish rewards.
translations.idiomatic: And so brave soldiers who are first to scale the walls in an assault, who smash through enemy lines, cut down generals and seize their banners, who charge into showers of arrows and stones without flinching from fire or boiling water — they do all this because they are driven by the promise of lavish rewards.

sentences.zh: 其在閭巷少年，攻剽椎埋，劫人作姦，掘冢鑄幣，任俠并兼，借交報仇，篡逐幽隱，不避法禁，走死地如騖者，其實皆為財用耳。

translations.literal: As for the young men of the alleys and lanes who rob and murder, waylay and commit crimes, dig up graves and forge coins, act as knights-errant absorbing smaller rivals, avenge wrongs through borrowed connections, usurp and pursue in dark concealment, defying all laws and prohibitions, rushing toward death as eagerly as ducks taking to water — in truth, they all do so for the sake of wealth.
translations.idiomatic: As for the young ruffians of the back alleys — those who rob and murder, waylay travelers, forge coins and dig up graves, act as knights-errant swallowing up rivals, borrow connections to exact revenge, and carry out covert schemes in the shadows, defying every law and prohibition, rushing toward their own deaths as eagerly as ducks taking flight — in truth, they do it all for the sake of money.

sentences.zh: 今夫趙女鄭姬，設形容，揳鳴琴，揄長袂，躡利屣，目挑心招，出不遠千里，不擇老少者，奔富厚也。

translations.literal: Now consider the beauties of Zhao and the maidens of Zheng: they adorn their faces, set aside their singing zithers, trail their long sleeves, and trip along in fine slippers, beckoning with their eyes and luring with their hearts, traveling a thousand li without regard for whether a man is old or young — all for the sake of running toward wealth.
translations.idiomatic: Consider the beauties of Zhao and the maidens of Zheng. They adorn their faces, take up the singing zither, trail their long sleeves, and trip along in fine slippers, beckoning with glances and luring with their hearts. They will travel a thousand li, caring nothing whether a man is old or young — all for the sake of wealth.

sentences.zh: 游閒公子，飾冠劍，連車騎，亦為富貴容也。

translations.literal: The idle young lords who adorn their caps and swords and parade in linked carriages and mounted escorts also do so to display the appearance of wealth and nobility.
translations.idiomatic: The idle young lords who adorn their caps and swords and parade about in linked carriages with mounted escorts do so to put on the trappings of wealth and nobility.

sentences.zh: 弋射漁獵，犯晨夜，冒霜雪，馳阬谷，不避猛獸之害，為得味也。

translations.literal: Those who hunt with arrows, fish, and pursue game, braving the dawn and night, enduring frost and snow, galloping through ravines and valleys, not shrinking from the danger of fierce beasts — they do so for the sake of obtaining fine flavors.
translations.idiomatic: Those who hunt with bow and arrow, fish, and track game, braving the dawn and the night, enduring frost and snow, galloping through ravines and valleys without shrinking from the danger of fierce beasts — they do it all for the sake of fine flavors.

sentences.zh: 博戲馳逐，鬬雞走狗，作色相矜，必爭勝者，重失負也。
醫方諸食技術之人，焦神極能，為重糈也。
吏士舞文弄法，刻章偽書，不避刀鋸之誅者，沒於賂遺也。
農工商賈畜長，固求富益貨也。
此有知盡能索耳，終不餘力而讓財矣。
諺曰：「百里不販樵，千里不販糴。」
居之一歲，種之以穀；
十歲，樹之以木；
百歲，來之以德。
德者，人物之謂也。
今有無秩祿之奉，爵邑之入，而樂與之比者。
命曰「素封」。
封者食租稅，歲率戶二百。
千戶之君則二十萬，朝覲聘享出其中。
庶民農工商賈，率亦歲萬息二千，百萬之家則二十萬，而更傜租賦出其中。
衣食之欲，恣所好美矣。
故曰陸地牧馬二百蹄，牛蹄角千，千足羊，澤中千足彘，水居千石魚陂，山居千章之材。
安邑千樹棗；
燕、秦千樹栗；
蜀、漢、江陵千樹橘；
淮北、常山已南，河濟之閒千樹萩；
陳、夏千畝漆；
齊、魯千畝桑麻；
渭川千畝竹；
及名國萬家之城，帶郭千畝畝鐘之田，若千畝卮茜，千畦薑韭：此其人皆與千戶侯等。
然是富給之資也，不窺市井，不行異邑，坐而待收，身有處士之義而取給焉。
若至家貧親老，妻子軟弱，歲時無以祭祀進醵，飲食被服不足以自通，如此不慚恥，則無所比矣。
是以無財作力，少有鬬智，既饒爭時，此其大經也。
今治生不待危身取給，則賢人勉焉。
是故本富為上，末富次之，姦富最下。
無巖處奇士之行，而長貧賤，好語仁義，亦足羞也。
凡編戶之民，富相什則卑下之，伯則畏憚之，千則役，萬則仆，物之理也。
夫用貧求富，農不如工，工不如商，刺繡文不如倚市門，此言末業，貧者之資也。

通邑大都，酤一歲千釀，醯醬千瓨，漿千甔，屠牛羊彘千皮，販穀糶千鐘，薪槁千車，船長千丈，木千章，竹竿萬，其軺車百乘，牛車千兩，木器髤者千枚，銅器千鈞，素木鐵器若炧茜千石，馬蹄蹾千，牛千足，羊彘千雙，僮手指千，筋角丹沙千斤，其帛絮細布千鈞，文采千匹，榻布皮革千石，漆千斗，糱麹鹽豉千荅，鮐鮆千斤，鯫千石，鮑千鈞，棗栗千石者三之，狐鼦裘千皮，羔羊裘千石，旃席千具，佗果菜千鐘，子貸金錢千貫，節駔會，貪賈三之，廉賈五之，此亦比千乘之家，其大率也。
佗雜業不中什二，則非吾財也。
請略道當世千里之中，賢人所以富者，令後世得以觀擇焉。
蜀卓氏之先，趙人也，用鐵冶富。
秦破趙，遷卓氏。
卓氏見虜略，獨夫妻推輦，行詣遷處。
諸遷虜少有餘財，爭與吏，求近處，處葭萌。
唯卓氏曰：「此地狹薄。
吾聞汶山之下，沃野，下有蹲鴟，至死不饑。
民工於市，易賈。」
乃求遠遷。
致之臨邛，大喜，即鐵山鼓鑄，運籌策，傾滇蜀之民，富至僮千人。
田池射獵之樂，擬於人君。
程鄭，山東遷虜也，亦冶鑄，賈椎髻之民，富埒卓氏，俱居臨邛。
宛孔氏之先，梁人也，用鐵冶為業。
秦伐魏，遷孔氏南陽。
大鼓鑄，規陂池，連車騎，游諸侯，因通商賈之利，有游閒公子之賜與名。
然其贏得過當，愈於纖嗇，家致富數千金，故南陽行賈盡法孔氏之雍容。
魯人俗儉嗇，而曹邴氏尤甚，以鐵冶起，富至巨萬。
然家自父兄子孫約，俛有拾，仰有取，貰貸行賈遍郡國。
鄒、魯以其故多去文學而趨利者，以曹邴氏也。
齊俗賤奴虜，而刀閒獨愛貴之。
桀黠奴，人之所患也，唯刀閒收取，使之逐漁鹽商賈之利，或連車騎，交守相，然愈益任之。
終得其力，起富數千萬。
故曰「寧爵毋刀」，言其能使豪奴自饒而盡其力。
周人既纖，而師史尤甚，轉轂以百數，賈郡國，無所不至。
洛陽街居在齊秦楚趙之中，貧人學事富家，相矜以久賈，數過邑不入門，設任此等，故師史能致七千萬。
宣曲任氏之先，為督道倉吏。
秦之敗也，豪傑皆爭取金玉，而任氏獨窖倉粟。
楚漢相距滎陽也，民不得耕種，米石至萬，而豪傑金玉盡歸任氏，任氏以此起富。
富人爭奢侈，而任氏折節為儉，力田畜。
田畜人爭取賤賈，任氏獨取貴善。
富者數世。
然任公家約，非田畜所出弗衣食，公事不畢則身不得飲酒食肉。
以此為閭里率，故富而主上重之。
塞之斥也，唯橋姚已致馬千匹，牛倍之，羊萬頭，粟以萬鐘計。
吳楚七國兵起時，長安中列侯封君行從軍旅，齎貸子錢，子錢家以為侯邑國在關東，關東成敗未決，莫肯與。
唯無鹽氏出捐千金貸，其息什之。
三月，吳楚平，一歲之中，則無鹽氏之息什倍，用此富埒關中。
關中富商大賈，大抵盡諸田，田嗇、田蘭。
韋家栗氏，安陵、杜杜氏，亦巨萬。
此其章章尤異者也。
皆非有爵邑奉祿弄法犯姦而富，盡椎埋去就，與時俯仰，獲其贏利，以末致財，用本守之，以武一切，用文持之，變化有概，故足術也。
若至力農畜，工虞商賈，為權利以成富，大者傾郡，中者傾縣，下者傾鄉里者，不可勝數。
夫纖嗇筋力，治生之正道也，而富者必用奇勝。
田農，掘業，而秦揚以蓋一州。
掘冢，姦事也，而田叔以起。
博戲，惡業也，而桓發用富。
行賈，丈夫賤行也，而雍樂成以饒。
販脂，辱處也，而雍伯千金。
賣漿，小業也，而張氏千萬。
灑削，薄技也，而郅氏鼎食。
胃脯，簡微耳，濁氏連騎。
馬醫，淺方，張裏擊鐘。
此皆誠壹之所致。
由是觀之，富無經業，則貨無常主，能者輻湊，不肖者瓦解。
千金之家比一都之君，巨萬者乃與王者同樂。
豈所謂「素封」者邪？
非也？
